Graph Dataset Analysis

Practical Shuffle Coding Edge Models — Kunze et al., NeurIPS 2024

Analyzing 16 graph datasets across 3 sources (SZIP, REC, TU). Comparing Erdos-Renyi, Preferential Attachment, Configuration Model, and Hollywood (Pitman-Yor) edge models.

Contents

  • The Four Edge Models (explainer)
  • Overview & Comparison Plots
  • Graph Statistics Table
  • Model Comparison (bits per edge)
  • Degree Distributions: Empirical vs Models
  • Key Findings

The Four Edge Models

We compare four models of increasing expressiveness for how edges form in a graph. Each model assigns a probability to any given graph — higher probability = fewer bits needed to encode it. The tradeoff: more expressive models fit the data better, but cost more bits to describe the model itself.

How each model generates a graph (numbers = node degree)

Erdos-Renyi (ER)
P(edge) = p for all pairs, independently.
Predicts Poisson degrees — symmetric bell curve, no hubs.
Preferential Attachment (PA)
New nodes connect to existing ones with P ∝ degree.
Predicts P(k) ~ k-3 — power law, but slope is always -3.
Hollywood / Pitman-Yor (PY)
Edge endpoints assigned via Chinese Restaurant Process:
P(existing node) ∝ degree - α,   P(new node) ∝ θ + Kα
Predicts P(k) ~ k-(1+α) — power law with tunable slope.
Configuration Model
Takes the exact degree sequence as input, then randomly wires stubs.
Does not predict a degree distribution — it matches whatever you give it. The cost is encoding the full degree sequence.

What each model predicts for the degree distribution

Left (linear): ER’s Poisson is a symmetric hump. PA and Hollywood are heavily skewed toward low degrees with long tails.
Right (log-log): Power laws appear as straight lines. ER curves downward (exponential tail cutoff). PA is always slope -3. Hollywood’s slope is tunable via α. Config (green dots) can match any shape — it’s the data itself.

Hollywood is so much worse it distorts the scale. Here’s the same chart zoomed to just the three competitive models:

Degree Distributions: Empirical vs Model Predictions

Each plot shows the empirical degree distribution (black/green dots) overlaid with predictions from four models:
ER (Binomial/Poisson — no heavy tail) • PA (Preferential Attachment, fixed k-3 power law) • Hollywood (Pitman-Yor, tunable k-(1+α)) • Config (= empirical by construction). Left panel: empirical + overlays. Right panel: model predictions compared.

SZIP Single Large Graphs

REC Large Social & Non-Social Networks

Large REC graphs (Foursquare, Digg, YouTube, Skitter) — degree distribution only (too large for PY simulation):


REC / Digg

REC / Foursquare

REC / Skitter

REC / YouTube

TU Graph Collections (aggregate degree distribution)

Key Findings

Configuration Model wins in 11/12 datasets

The configuration model consistently compresses better than ER by 0.5–5 bits/edge. It captures degree heterogeneity — real graphs have highly non-uniform degree distributions (high variance, skew, often heavy tails), and encoding the degree sequence explicitly pays off.

Hollywood (Pitman-Yor) model is too restrictive

With only 2 parameters (alpha, theta), the PY process cannot capture the specific shape of empirical degree distributions. The cost of a poor parametric fit to the degree sequence far outweighs the savings from having fewer parameters. For many datasets, the optimizer finds alpha ≈ 0 and very large theta, effectively defaulting to a Dirichlet process — a poor model for heterogeneous degrees.

Preferential Attachment (PA): right idea, wrong exponent

PA predicts a fixed k-3 power law. The overlay plots show it captures the right shape for heavy-tailed networks (straight line on log-log) but the slope is often wrong — real networks have exponents ranging from ~1.7 to ~3.0. PA also can’t model the low-degree bump or high-degree cutoff seen in most empirical distributions. It sits between ER (too narrow) and Hollywood (tunable but still too rigid) in expressiveness.

Most graphs are NOT convincingly power-law

When tested against the lognormal alternative using the Clauset et al. methodology, most datasets show low p-values (power-law is not significantly better than lognormal). Only 'geom', 'as' (SZIP), 'Gowalla', and 'Skitter' (REC) show evidence of genuinely heavy-tailed degree distributions.

Dataset characteristics summary

  • SZIP (6 graphs): Medium-sized single graphs (332–25K nodes). Moderate to sparse. Some heavy-tailed.
  • REC (6 graphs): Very large single graphs (196K–3.2M nodes). Extremely sparse. Social networks show extreme degree heterogeneity (max degree up to 106K).
  • TU (4 collections): Collections of small graphs. MUTAG (molecules): narrow bounded degrees. IMDB-BINARY: dense ego-networks. PROTEINS: biological. reddit_threads: star-like trees.

Generated from the shuffle-coding datasets. Analysis code: analyze_graphs.py | Report: generate_report.py
